insight: In Prismata, there is theoretically one optimal winning line for any given set, but since that line is almost never executed perfectly, a suboptimal strategy played perfectly can still beat the optimal strategy played poorly. This means player skill in execution matters as much as set-reading.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion about whether 'econ mains' or playstyle preferences are valid in a deterministic game.

insight: If a player has stronger skills in high-economy games, they should steer borderline sets toward higher economy rather than playing purely to the theoretical optimum, since exploiting personal skill advantages can be correct play.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion about whether playstyle preferences ('econ main') are meaningful in Prismata's deterministic fixed-pool format.

insight: Studying BSO+1 (base set only plus one advanced unit) can build instincts for specific strategic questions in sets containing that unit—such as when to build Blastforge or Conduit, or whether to buy Tarsier on a given turn—even if it does not provide definitive answers, since BSO itself is not fully solved.
author: .jrkirby
context: Discussion on the value and limits of BSO+1 analysis as a study method.

insight: In early base set theory, early Conduit was believed to potentially be a P1 win, until P2 found a Rhino hold that set up an exploit, shifting the assessment.
author: namington
context: Brief historical note at the end of a long meta discussion thread about base set opening theory.

insight: Apollo was tried at 4 supply and was considered bad at that cost; community members speculated that even 10 supply Apollo would likely not be bought in a base+Apollo set, suggesting Apollo's value is highly dependent on the broader set composition providing good snipe targets.
author: consensus
context: Humorous discussion about hypothetical 10-supply Apollo sets that evolved into a genuine analysis of Apollo's set-dependence.

insight: Apollo's self-synergy argument is: if you are buying Apollo, it must be because the opponent has good Apollo targets; therefore, additional Apollos are also good in the same set. However, mrguy888 counters that each subsequent Apollo snipes a progressively worse target than the previous one, limiting the synergy.
author: amalloy
context: Discussion about whether multiple Apollos have self-synergy in a hypothetical high-supply Apollo set.

insight: Savior is considered by experts to be underrated—stronger than most players think—and players at lower skill levels are noted to be particularly bad at utilizing Savior correctly.
author: masn6811
context: Brief exchange where a player asks if Savior is secretly very strong and masn6811 confirms it is pretty good while agreeing players misuse it.

insight: Tarsier is almost certainly the most-purchased unit in Prismata after Drone, Engineer, and tech buildings, supporting the community axiom that 'every set is a Tarsier set.'
author: namington
context: Discussion about playstyle mains and the ubiquity of certain units across all sets.

insight: 'BSO' stands for Base Set Only, referring to games or analysis using only the default Prismata units without any advanced random set units. 'BSO+1' refers to base set plus one advanced unit, used as a simplified study framework.
author: consensus
context: Multiple messages across threads referencing BSO and BSO+1 as established community shorthand for set analysis.
